summary: Independently verify the external review claims: WISE-Flow, GRASP, judge-AUROC paper, and venue dates

wiseflow.title: WISE-Flow: Workflow-Induced Structured Experience for Self-Evolving Conversational Service Agents
wiseflow.arxiv_id_or_url: arXiv:2601.08158 (https://arxiv.org/abs/2601.08158) — the reviewer's claimed id is CORRECT and exact. Reviewer's name "WISE-Flow" is also correct.
wiseflow.date_venue: Submitted January 13, 2026 (arXiv preprint, v1). No published venue indicated on the abstract page as of 2026-07-06.
wiseflow.method_summary: WISE-Flow is a training-free (no gradient updates) framework that converts historical agent interaction logs into reusable "workflows" — procedural experience with prerequisite-augmented action blocks. Offline, it collects full logs (dialog, tool traces, environment feedback) and partitions each task's trajectories into three outcome categories: clean successes (no tool-call errors), recovered successes (errors that later recover), and failures. Instead of clustering failures, it builds CONTRASTIVE PAIRS — pairing a clean success with a recovered success or a failure and concatenating them into a "contrastive block" that highlights outcome-determining differences. An LLM "inducer" then runs a three-pass procedure per task/workflow: (i) analysis identifies the goal-consistent action sequence and the key environment feedback explaining failures; (ii) drafting synthesizes a candidate workflow with explicit steps and per-action prerequisites; (iii) reflection-and-revision validates each step/prerequisite/branch AGAINST THE SOURCE TRAJECTORIES, removing or fixing unsupported or non-executable recommendations. This validation is purely LLM self-reflection cross-checked against the logs — there is no A/B test, no paired comparison on held-out/matched tasks, no significance threshold, and no no-regression gate. At deployment, the task query is embedded and used to retrieve top-K workflows by embedding similarity; the agent performs "progress alignment" (matching its last successful tool call to planned steps) and then "prerequisite-aware feasibility checking" against current environment state before executing a suggested action. Injection is thus conditional on runtime state feasibility, not on any validated performance benefit. Data are synthetic benchmark rollouts (10 simulated independent trials per task) on ToolSandbox and τ²-bench, with environment ground-truth (tool-call success/failure, state transitions, milestone completion) as the signal. Reported gains: ToolSandbox Claude 3.7 Sonnet SR 95% / F_β 0.9745 / missed-milestone 2.46%; Qwen3-235B-A22B SR 88% / F_β 0.9615 / MMR 3.69%; τ²-bench Claude 3.7 Sonnet pass^1 0.564 / F_β 0.6936; one-shot success 85% vs 71.63% for raw logs. The paper makes NO cost/economics argument and NO off-policy-vs-on-policy comparison.
wiseflow.data_and_signal: Data: SYNTHETIC benchmark logs, not real customer-service logs. The authors "simulate 10 independent trials to collect the raw experience for each task" inside the ToolSandbox and τ²-bench simulators. (Reviewer's phrase "historical customer-service logs" is a loose characterization — τ²-bench is a customer-service-style domain, but the logs are self-generated simulator rollouts, not deployment traffic.) Feedback signal: environment ground-truth from the simulators — tool-call success/failure, environment state transitions, and milestone completion — used to LABEL trajectories as clean-success / recovered-success / failure. There is NO learned verifier, NO LLM judge for admission, and NO statistical test; the ground-truth labels feed contrastive pairing and LLM self-reflection only. The same base LLM is reused at deployment for prerequisite feasibility checking. Training-free confirmed (no fine-tuning; LLM synthesis + retrieval only).
wiseflow.benchmarks_results: Benchmarks: ToolSandbox and τ²-bench (the reviewer's claim that it is evaluated on τ²-bench is CONFIRMED; ToolSandbox is an additional benchmark). Base models: Claude 3.7 Sonnet and Qwen3-235B-A22B. Results (WISE-Flow, reported as best vs baselines incl. REMEMBERER/raw-logs): ToolSandbox / Claude 3.7 Sonnet — Success 95%, F_β 0.9745, Missed-Milestone 2.46%. ToolSandbox / Qwen3-235B-A22B — Success 88%, F_β 0.9615, MMR 3.69%. τ²-bench / Claude 3.7 Sonnet — pass^1 0.564, F_β 0.6936, MMR 30.75%. Efficiency note: one-shot success 85% vs 71.63% for raw logs on ToolSandbox. No GPU-hours/token/cost budget reported (authors state in A.3.2 they did not log aggregate usage).
wiseflow.overlap_with_replay: Point-by-point against REPLAY v1: (1) "From interaction logs" — OVERLAP. Both mine agent interaction logs (dialog + tool traces + environment feedback) as the raw material for building an experience library, and both are training-free. (2) "Cluster failures" — NO OVERLAP. WISE-Flow does NOT cluster failures at all (confirmed: no clustering anywhere). It instead partitions trajectories by outcome (clean/recovered/failure) and forms CONTRASTIVE PAIRS. (3) "Propose experience-library entries one cluster at a time" — PARTIAL/WEAK OVERLAP. It does propose reusable experience entries (workflows with per-action prerequisites), and induction is organized per-task, but there is no cluster-by-cluster procedure and the entry format is a procedural workflow rather than a failure-mode-keyed library entry. (4) "Validate each entry via targeted paired A/B on cluster-matched tasks with a pessimistic statistical admission gate" — NO OVERLAP (this is the sharpest divergence). WISE-Flow's only validation is offline LLM self-reflection cross-checked against the source trajectories ("reflection and revision validates each step...against trajectories, removing or fixing unsupported or non-executable recommendations"). There is NO A/B test, NO paired comparison, NO cluster-matched evaluation set, NO significance test, and NO pessimistic/statistical admission gate. (5) "No-harm control" — NO OVERLAP. There is no mechanism that blocks admitting/injecting an entry that hurts performance; the paper explicitly lists guidance-induced harmful tool calls as an unmitigated limitation and only proposes confirmation steps as future work. (6) "Conditional injection" — PARTIAL OVERLAP but different conditioning. WISE-Flow injects conditionally via runtime "prerequisite-aware feasibility checking" against environment state — i.e., conditioned on whether a step is currently executable, NOT conditioned on statistically validated benefit as in REPLAY. (7) Headline "logs provide validation economics" — NO OVERLAP. The paper makes no cost/economics argument, no off-policy-vs-on-policy comparison, and no budget-matched validation claim (it does not even log its own compute). (8) "Tested on τ²-bench with hidden-verifier protocol" — PARTIAL OVERLAP. τ²-bench is shared, but WISE-Flow uses standard simulator ground-truth/milestone metrics, not a hidden-verifier evaluation protocol.
wiseflow.surviving_differentiation: REPLAY v1 retains strong, specific novelty over WISE-Flow — be blunt: nearly every load-bearing REPLAY mechanism is ABSENT here. Surviving claims: (A) Failure-driven CLUSTERING of logs into recurring failure modes and building the library one cluster at a time — WISE-Flow has zero clustering (it uses outcome partitioning + contrastive pairing). (B) The entire targeted PAIRED A/B VALIDATION of each candidate on cluster-matched tasks — WISE-Flow does no empirical per-entry validation whatsoever; its "validation" is LLM self-consistency against the same logs it was induced from, which is exactly the kind of unfalsified self-reflection REPLAY's statistical gate is designed to reject. (C) The PESSIMISTIC STATISTICAL ADMISSION GATE (significance/lower-bound thresholding before admission) — entirely absent. (D) The explicit NO-HARM / no-regression control that prevents net-negative entries from being injected — absent and openly acknowledged as an open risk in their limitations. (E) The HEADLINE "validation economics" thesis — that reusing off-policy logs makes per-entry validation cheap relative to on-policy rollouts, demonstrated via a budget-matched off-policy-vs-on-policy comparison — is completely unaddressed; WISE-Flow neither frames validation as an economic problem nor runs any budget-matched or off-vs-on-policy experiment (it doesn't even report its compute). (F) The hidden-verifier evaluation protocol on τ²-bench. Caveats to stay honest: WISE-Flow DOES preempt the generic framing of "training-free distillation of reusable textual/procedural experience from agent logs, evaluated on τ²-bench, with conditional/feasibility-gated injection." So REPLAY should NOT claim novelty on "use logs to build a training-free experience library for τ²-bench agents" in the abstract — that overlaps. REPLAY's defensible contribution is specifically the VALIDATION MACHINERY (cluster-matched paired A/B + pessimistic admission gate + no-harm control) and the VALIDATION-ECONOMICS claim, none of which WISE-Flow has. Also note the reviewer slightly mischaracterized WISE-Flow's data as "historical customer-service logs" — they are synthetic simulator rollouts (10 trials/task), which weakens any WISE-Flow real-log claim and is a further point of differentiation if REPLAY uses genuine interaction logs.

grasp.title: GRASP: Gated Regression-Aware Skill Proposer for Self-Improving LLM Agents
grasp.arxiv_id_or_url: arXiv:2605.29668 — https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29668 (reviewer's ID is CORRECT; the "GRASP" name is also correct — full title "Gated Regression-Aware Skill Proposer". Note there is an unrelated same-name paper at 2605.16598 "GRASP: Graph Agentic Search over Propositions" — not this one.)
grasp.date_venue: Submitted to arXiv 28 May 2026 (cs; preprint, v1). No published venue stated in the paper; clinical/medical-agent framing (authors from a med-AI group incl. Rueckert/Bressem).
grasp.method_summary: GRASP treats agent self-improvement as a sequence of edits to a bounded "skill library" (structured behavioral notes), where each candidate edit is admitted only if it produces net improvement on a balanced held-out probe under a hard regression budget. Per training batch, the agent is run on a batch of dev-split episodes; failing traces are labeled by an LLM classifier with open-vocabulary, mechanism-specific failure modes (e.g. `date_filter_omitted`, not `wrong_answer`) and grouped by label with no minimum group size. A skill-writer LLM then generates K=4 candidate edits — ADD / MODIFY / REMOVE (modification and removal are first-class, so the library evolves rather than only grows) — by cycling over failure-mode groups largest-to-smallest. Each of the K+1 candidate libraries (K proposals plus the current library) is re-evaluated on a balanced probe of ~36 episodes containing up to N/2 previously-failing and N/2 previously-passing samples, stratified by task type, drawn from earlier episodes in the current epoch. The admission gate counts fixes F(c) on previously-failing samples and regressions R(c) on previously-passing samples, and accepts the highest-scoring candidate only if (F(c)−F₀)−(R(c)−R₀)>0 AND R(c)≤R₀ (net gain plus no new regressions beyond baseline). If an accepted candidate still causes some regressions, a contrastive revision step re-invokes the skill-writer with the regressing traces to produce a narrower version; regressions from invalid agent actions are up-weighted (λ=2) in the score though the hard budget uses unweighted counts. Training runs 5 epochs over the dev split at batch size 48. Validation is against real environment reward: exact-match accuracy against ground-truth answers and actual FHIR-server state, not an LLM judge or hidden verifier.
grasp.data_and_signal: Data source = ON-POLICY BENCHMARK ROLLOUTS, not real interaction logs. All training/probe data are fresh agent rollouts on the development split of benchmark tasks ("Probe examples are drawn exclusively from the development split, with validation and test examples never entering training"; 5 epochs, batch 48, 36-episode probe). There is NO reuse of pre-existing/production logs — the words "log", "logs", "off-policy", "production trace", "reuse" do not appear in a methodological sense. Feedback signal = REAL VERIFIER / real reward: exact-match accuracy against ground-truth answers and ground-truth FHIR-server state (objective pass/fail), for both the failure labeling inputs and the probe admission gate. An LLM is used only for two auxiliary roles — a failure-mode classifier and the skill-writer/reviser — never as the reward or the gate. Bootstrap 95% CIs / permutation p-values / Cohen's d appear ONLY in Appendix E.4 as post-hoc significance reporting of final results, and are NOT part of the admission gate.
grasp.benchmarks_results: Benchmarks: MedAgentBench (FHIR/EHR clinical agent tasks) as primary, plus MedAgentBench-v2, FHIR-AgentBench, and AgentBench sub-tasks (ALFWorld, WebShop, DBBench, OS Interaction) for generalization. NOT evaluated on tau2-bench and no hidden-verifier protocol. Base models (5): gpt-oss-120b, DeepSeek V4 Flash, Gemini 3.1 Flash Lite, GPT-4.1, GPT-5.4 (low reasoning). Headline: on MedAgentBench, gpt-oss-120b rises 40.6% → 88.8%, beating the strongest of five self-improvement baselines (Evo-MedAgent 67.8%) by +21.0 points; all five models improve by +17.2 to +40.3 points. Matched-compute ablation (Table 4): selecting the applied edit WITHOUT probe scores reaches only 70.8% (skill-writer's preference) and 67.2% (random), vs 88.8% with the full gated method — i.e., the gain comes from validated (gated) editing, not extra compute or better writing.
grasp.overlap_with_replay:
Point-by-point vs REPLAY v1:
(1) Log failure-clustering — PARTIAL OVERLAP but different data: GRASP does cluster failures (LLM classifier → mechanism-specific labels → groups), matching REPLAY's clustering step. BUT the failures come from fresh on-policy dev-split rollouts, NOT from pre-existing real interaction logs. REPLAY's premise of mining already-collected logs is absent.
(2) Per-entry candidates one cluster at a time — OVERLAP: GRASP cycles over failure-mode groups largest-to-smallest and emits K=4 ADD/MODIFY/REMOVE candidate skill-library edits, close to "propose experience-library entries one cluster at a time" (though it batches K candidates per update and picks the best, rather than strictly one).
(3) Targeted paired micro-validation on cluster-matched tasks — PARTIAL / WEAKER OVERLAP: GRASP re-runs candidates on a balanced held-out probe of previously-failing + previously-passing samples stratified BY TASK TYPE. This is a paired fail/pass validation, but it is a GLOBAL balanced probe, NOT specifically matched to the individual candidate's failure cluster. So REPLAY's "cluster-matched" targeting of the validation set is not what GRASP does.
(4) Pessimistic STATISTICAL admission gate — NOT OVERLAPPING as stated: GRASP's gate is DETERMINISTIC RAW-COUNT ARITHMETIC, not statistical. Exact rule: accept only if (F(c)−F₀)−(R(c)−R₀)>0 AND R(c)≤R₀. There is NO confidence bound, NO pessimistic lower bound, NO hypothesis test in the gate (Wilson/Bernstein/lower-bound not present; bootstrap CIs appear only for post-hoc result reporting in Appendix E.4, not the gate). It is also NOT text-reflective — no LLM judges the candidate; admission is on objective exact-match pass/fail counts against real reward.
(5) No-harm control — OVERLAP (conceptually): the hard regression budget R(c)≤R₀ (no new regressions beyond baseline, invalid-action regressions up-weighted λ=2) is a genuine no-harm/regression-safety control, matching REPLAY's no-harm control in spirit — this is the strongest overlap and does occupy the "don't silently regress prior correct behavior" idea.
(6) Conditional injection — OVERLAP: only gated candidates are admitted into the library; rejects are dropped or narrowed via contrastive revision.
(7) "Logs provide validation economics" headline + budget-matched off-policy-vs-on-policy comparison — NOT PRESENT: GRASP makes no validation-economics claim; "off-policy/on-policy/validation economics/sample efficiency" never appear. Its ONLY budget-matched study is matched-COMPUTE (probe-scored vs unscored selection), and BOTH arms are on-policy fresh rollouts — there is no off-policy (log-reuse) vs on-policy comparison. GRASP's Limitations even call the probe "the dominant cost," the opposite of a log-reuse-is-cheap thesis.
grasp.surviving_differentiation:
REPLAY can still cleanly claim, and GRASP does NOT do, the following (be specific):
(A) OFF-POLICY LOG REUSE AS THE CORE MECHANISM: REPLAY learns from and validates against pre-existing REAL interaction logs; GRASP is entirely on-policy on benchmark dev-split rollouts and never touches logs. This is the biggest surviving gap.
(B) THE "VALIDATION ECONOMICS" THESIS + BUDGET-MATCHED OFF-VS-ON-POLICY EXPERIMENT: REPLAY's headline is that reusing logs makes validation cheap, and it backs this with a budget-matched off-policy-vs-on-policy comparison. GRASP has neither the claim nor the experiment (its matched-compute ablation is on-policy vs on-policy); GRASP explicitly frames its probe as expensive.
(C) A GENUINELY STATISTICAL, PESSIMISTIC ADMISSION GATE: GRASP's gate is a raw fixes-minus-regressions count with a hard zero-new-regression budget — deterministic, no confidence bound, no pessimism/lower-bound, no paired significance test in the loop. REPLAY's pessimistic statistical gate (confidence-bound / lower-bound admission) is a real methodological distinction; do NOT concede the "statistical admission gate" contribution to GRASP.
(D) CLUSTER-MATCHED / TARGETED PAIRED VALIDATION: GRASP validates on a single global balanced probe stratified only by task type, not on tasks matched to each candidate's specific failure cluster. REPLAY's per-entry, cluster-matched targeted micro-validation is narrower/more targeted.
(E) tau2-bench + HIDDEN-VERIFIER PROTOCOL: GRASP is a clinical/FHIR (MedAgentBench-family) + AgentBench story with a visible exact-match verifier; it never uses tau2-bench and has no hidden-verifier evaluation.
CAVEATS / where REPLAY should NOT over-claim: the general shape "cluster failures → propose bounded-library edits (add/modify/remove) → validate each edit on a held-out fail/pass probe → admit only on net improvement with a no-harm regression constraint → conditional injection" is genuinely and closely anticipated by GRASP. In particular the NO-HARM / regression-safety control (item 5 above) and the paired previously-failing/previously-passing probe are strong prior art — REPLAY must foreground the log-reuse/validation-economics/off-vs-on-policy axis and the statistical (not count-arithmetic) nature of its gate as the load-bearing novelty, and should not present "gate that prevents regressions on a balanced probe" or "failure-clustered library edits" as itself novel.

judgeAuroc.title: VERIFIED PAPER (id given by reviewer): "Catching One in Five: LLM-as-Judge Blind Spots in Production Multi-Turn Transaction Agents" (Sawyer Zhang, Alexander Wang, Sophie Lei). NOTE: this paper does NOT contain the AUROC=0.65 claim. The reviewer's AUROC claim actually belongs to a DIFFERENT paper: "From Confident Closing to Silent Failure: Characterizing False Success in LLM Agents" (Laksh Advani).
judgeAuroc.arxiv_id_or_url: Reviewer-cited id verified: arXiv:2606.10315 (https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.10315). Actual source of the AUROC=0.65 / tau2-bench numbers: arXiv:2606.09863 (https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.09863).
judgeAuroc.date_venue: 2606.10315: arXiv preprint, cs.CL, submitted 9 Jun 2026, no conference venue. 2606.09863 (the real AUROC source): arXiv preprint, cs.LG, dated 1 Jun 2026; associated with FAGEN@ICML2026 workshop per indexing.
judgeAuroc.method_summary: CLAIM VERIFICATION OUTCOME: the reviewer's attribution is WRONG. arXiv:2606.10315 ("Catching One in Five") studies a deployed multi-turn food-and-beverage ordering agent (Singapore coffee brand, bilingual EN/Mandarin, 5-9 user turns across 34 scenario families) and evaluates its built-in LLM judge purely on RECALL of human-confirmed defects, NOT AUROC. It reports the judge surfaces only ~18-25% of systematic defects (2 of 9 patterns = 22% in batch B1; 0 of 23 defects surfaced operationally, 0/100 rounds flagged in B2; ~18% pooled), and that the judge catches turn-local errors but misses cross-turn state issues (cart hallucination, confirm-gate lockout, stale referents). It reports NO AUROC/AUC/ROC number anywhere. The "AUROC barely above 0.65" figure comes instead from arXiv:2606.09863 ("From Confident Closing to Silent Failure", Laksh Advani), which analyzes 9,876 tau2-bench trajectories (8 model families) plus 1,879 AppWorld trajectories (4 families) with text-independent ground-truth of environment state. Advani sweeps 5 judge models x 5 prompt conditions x 3 prompt phrasings PLUS a strong baseline that is handed the full ground-truth task specification, and finds NO configuration exceeds AUROC 0.65 for false-success (FS) vs true-success (TS) detection on tau2-bench; reasoning models do not help (DeepSeek-R1 peaks 0.573, o3-mini 0.554). A lightweight task-disjoint TF-IDF detector instead reaches AUROC 0.825 on tau2-bench and 0.953 on AppWorld, recovering 4-8x more false successes than the best judge at equal flag rate with ~3,300x lower latency. Both papers converge on the same message: LLM judges are unreliable success/quality signals for multi-turn tool-using agents, especially for cross-turn/state failures.
judgeAuroc.data_and_signal: 2606.10315: real production logs (randomized multi-turn conversations from a live SE-Asian F&B ordering agent), signal = human-confirmed defect ground truth vs the deployed built-in LLM judge's pass/fail bits (recall as the metric). 2606.09863: agent trajectories from public benchmarks (tau2-bench 9,876 traj / 8 model families; AppWorld 1,879 traj / 4 families), signal = programmatic/environment-state ground truth for true-success vs false-success, used to score LLM judges (AUROC) and a TF-IDF baseline.
judgeAuroc.benchmarks_results: 2606.10315 (NO AUROC): recall of built-in judge = 22% (2/9 patterns, B1), 0% operational (0/23 defects, 0/100 rounds, B2), ~18% pooled; 125/220 rounds got suspected_bug notes but 0/220 became operational failures; Rogan-Gladen correction implies ~6-33x undercount. Domain = production F&B transaction agent. 2606.09863 (the AUROC source, tau2-bench + AppWorld): NO judge config exceeds AUROC 0.65 on FS-vs-TS (across 5 judge models x 5 prompt conditions x 3 phrasings + full-ground-truth-spec baseline); DeepSeek-R1 0.573, o3-mini 0.554; TF-IDF detector AUROC 0.825 (tau2-bench, task-disjoint) and 0.953 (AppWorld). EXTRA SWEEP (one-liners): (a) arXiv:2606.09863 Advani FAGEN@ICML2026 = the definitive tau2-bench judge-AUROC<=0.65 result (above). (b) AgentRewardBench, Lu et al., arXiv:2504.08942 (2025) = first benchmark judging LLM evaluators of web-agent trajectories, 1,302 expert-reviewed trajectories / 12 judges, finds no single judge excels everywhere; WebJudge precision ~73.7-82.0%. (c) TRACER (ICML 2026) = trajectory-level uncertainty metric that improves failure-detection AUROC by up to 37% over strongest baselines on tau2-bench/ToolHop/ComplexFuncBench, implying baseline judge AUROC is low. (d) "Evaluating Automatic Evaluations of Web Agent Trajectories" (OpenReview fQcUZMPIvu, the AgentRewardBench venue record) corroborates rule-based/judge underreporting of agent success.
judgeAuroc.overlap_with_replay: REFRAMING GUIDANCE for G2 judge AUC=0.68 (this schema field is repurposed as requested). Best move: cite arXiv:2606.09863 (Advani), NOT 2606.10315, for the headline. On tau2-bench — the exact benchmark REPLAY v1 uses — the strongest LLM-judge configuration (5 judge models x 5 prompts x 3 phrasings, even given the full ground-truth task spec) tops out below AUROC 0.65, and reasoning models are worse (0.573 / 0.554). Therefore our G2 judge at AUC=0.68 sits AT OR ABOVE the reported field ceiling for this task on this benchmark — 0.68 is domain-typical, arguably mildly favorable, not a weakness of REPLAY. This directly justifies REPLAY's core design: because single-shot judge point-estimates are near-chance on tau2-bench, we deliberately do NOT trust a judge score for admission and instead run paired A/B micro-validation with a pessimistic statistical admission gate + no-harm control — the "validation economics" claim is precisely the response to the judge-unreliability the literature documents. Supporting cite: AgentRewardBench (Lu et al. 2025, arXiv:2504.08942) for the general "no judge excels everywhere" finding. CRITICAL CAVEAT: do NOT cite 2606.10315 for the AUROC number — it reports recall (~18-25%), so a reviewer would catch a miscitation; attribute AUROC=0.65 strictly to 2606.09863. You MAY still cite 2606.10315 as complementary production evidence that multi-turn LLM judges miss >75% of real defects, reinforcing that judge-only success signals are unreliable in exactly REPLAY's multi-turn agent setting.
judgeAuroc.surviving_differentiation: Both papers are diagnostic (they measure judge weakness); neither builds anything resembling REPLAY. Neither does failure-clustering over interaction logs, neither proposes per-cluster experience-library candidate entries, neither performs targeted paired micro-validation with a pessimistic statistical admission gate + no-harm control, and neither makes a budget-matched off-policy-vs-on-policy "logs provide validation economics" argument. 2606.09863 offers only a cheap TF-IDF FALSE-SUCCESS detector as an alternative success signal — a drop-in replacement for the judge, not a library-optimization loop and not a validation-economics claim. So REPLAY's entire method is orthogonal and, if anything, these papers strengthen REPLAY's motivation. One honesty flag for us: 2606.09863's TF-IDF result (task-disjoint AUROC 0.83 on tau2-bench) shows a CHEAP non-judge signal can beat judges — a reviewer could ask why REPLAY relies on judge-based validation at all rather than such cheap detectors; we should pre-empt this by framing our statistical gate as signal-agnostic (it can consume any per-task success signal, including cheap detectors) rather than judge-dependent.

venues.iclr2027_status: NOT YET PUBLISHED. As of 2026-07-06 there is no official ICLR 2027 dates/CFP page on iclr.cc (iclr.cc/Conferences/2027 returns HTTP 404). Any "ICLR 2027" deadlines circulating (e.g. abstract Sep 19, 2026 / full paper Sep 24, 2026) are third-party PREDICTIONS extrapolated from prior years, NOT official. Beware fake conference listings (e.g. waset.org) claiming an "ICLR 2027 in Sydney" — these are not the real ICLR.
venues.iclr2026_reference_dates: Official (iclr.cc/Conferences/2026/Dates): Abstract submission deadline Sep 19, 2025 (AoE); Full paper submission deadline Sep 24, 2025 (AoE). Use this ~mid/late-September pattern to extrapolate an expected ICLR 2027 abstract deadline around Sep 18-19, 2026 and full paper around Sep 23-24, 2026 — but treat as unofficial until iclr.cc posts the 2027 page.
venues.icml2027_estimate: ICML 2026 official deadlines (icml.cc/Conferences/2026): abstract ~Jan 22-23, 2026 and full paper Jan 28, 2026 AoE (conference July 6-11, 2026, Seoul). Note ICML shifted earlier than the historical late-January/early-February norm. Extrapolating the ICML pattern, ICML 2027 full-paper deadline is expected around late January 2027 (roughly Jan 22-28, 2027), with abstract registration ~5-7 days prior (~mid/late January 2027). This is an ESTIMATE; ICML 2027 CFP is not yet published.
venues.aaai27_notification_dates: Official (aaai.org AAAI-27 Main Technical Track Call): Abstract deadline July 21, 2026; Full paper deadline July 28, 2026; PHASE 1 REJECTION NOTIFICATION September 24, 2026; Author feedback/rebuttal period October 19-25, 2026 (Phase 2 papers only); FINAL notification November 30, 2026. All AoE (UTC-12).
